title: Flown
description: Green water arrives from the river and comes to rest against the dam, held back yet never still. Ripples gather from the continuous inflow, carrying within them a quiet desire to move elsewhere. Clumps of water hyacinths appear and disappear across the surface, rising and sinking like a game of hide and seek. Their drifting forms compose a sequence of fleeting images, leaving the viewer to imagine the slow passage of floating and the currents that remain unseen..

sections.title: Prologue
sections.body: She moved through the house the way you move through memory — slowly, not quite touching things. Rooms that held years inside their walls. A window that let in the same light it always had.

sections.title: I. Threshold
sections.body: The door had been left open for so long that birds began treating the hallway as sky. This is what happens when you leave things: they become homes for other things. She stood at the edge of it. Both directions felt like leaving.

sections.title: II. Interior
sections.body: Every object carries a shadow of use. The cup that held morning. The chair that held waiting. To photograph a room is to make an argument — that this particular arrangement of things mattered once, briefly, completely.

sections.title: III. Light Source
sections.body: At 4pm the light came through the west window and made a rectangle on the floor she had been stepping around for thirty years. She finally stepped into it. Stood there until it moved.

sections.title: Coda
sections.body: What we keep is not what we remember. What we photograph is not what we saw. The book you hold is a room you cannot enter — only imagine having left.

title: 54 Rooms
description: A photographic census of rental rooms occupied across three cities over a decade. No people, only evidence of living. Beds unmade. Windows showing whatever city this happened to be. The rooms accumulate into a portrait of displacement as ordinary life.

sections.title: Delhi, 2013–2016
sections.body: The rooms in Lajpat Nagar all had the same yellow ceiling. I thought it was the building until I moved to Karol Bagh and found the same yellow. It was the light. It was always the light.

sections.title: Mumbai, 2016–2020
sections.body: In Mumbai the rooms are smaller but the windows are larger. You trade space for sky. This seems like a bad deal until you have lived without the sky long enough.

sections.title: Bengaluru, 2020–2023
sections.body: The pandemic rooms are the ones I cannot look at for long. They have a quality of arrested time. The others feel like departures. These feel like stays.

title: Border Radio
description: A 40-minute single-channel video work combining field recordings from the India-Pakistan border zone with testimonies of radio operators who manned the airwaves during the 1971 war. The static between stations as a form of mourning.

sections.title: Artist statement
sections.body: The static between stations as a form of mourning. The voices that come through as a form of archive. I spent two years recording along the border — not at the checkpoints but in the fields, the tea shops, the abandoned radio rooms.

sections.title: On the work
sections.body: What field recording teaches you is that silence is never empty. Border Radio tries to make audible what was never meant to be preserved — the ambient sound of a line that should not exist.

title: Monsoon Journal
description: A photo diary spanning seven monsoon seasons in North Kolkata — same streets, different light, different floods. Rain changes what the street is willing to show you. This book is the proof.

sections.title: Season one, 2017
sections.body: The first monsoon I photographed in North Kolkata I understood almost nothing of what I was seeing. I came back the next year. And the next.

sections.title: Season four, 2020
sections.body: By the fourth year I had stopped trying to make pictures. I walked and let the rain decide. What came out of the camera surprised me — not because it was unexpected but because it was true.

sections.title: Season seven, 2023
sections.body: The same street for seven years. The wall repainted three times. The chai stall that moved, came back, moved again. Rain as the only reliable thing.

title: The Quiet Industry
description: A photographic essay on the small workshops of Howrah — foundries, lathes, metal dust — made over three years. Industry at this scale has no spectacle. Only repetition, only the smell of metal, only hands.

sections.title: Preface
sections.body: Industry at this scale has no spectacle. Only repetition, only the smell of metal, only hands. I came to photograph Howrah because someone told me it was dying. Three years later I think they were wrong.

sections.title: The workshops
sections.body: Each workshop makes one thing. Bolts. Valves. Gear teeth. The precision required is extraordinary and entirely unacknowledged. The men who do this work have not been photographed.

title: A Long Season
description: Tracing seasonal change across a single field in Punjab over 18 months. A study in duration and attention. The work refuses a single vantage point — visitors move through the field as it moves through a year.

sections.title: Note on method
sections.body: I returned to the same field every three weeks for eighteen months, always at different times of day. The field is the same field. The light is never the same light.

sections.title: October
sections.body: The stubble burning begins in October. The sky turns the colour of old photographs. I have started to think of this book as a document of an agricultural practice that may not survive another generation.

title: Archive Fever
description: A two-city project working with photographic archives of Partition — images that were separated when families were separated. The archive is the wound. The exhibition does not heal it.

sections.title: On working with other people's photographs
sections.body: Every family photograph from before 1947 is an archive of what was lost. This project tries to reunite — digitally — images that were physically separated when borders were drawn through living rooms.

sections.title: The border as photographic event
sections.body: 1947 was also a photographic event. Millions of images crossed the border in suitcases, or didn't cross at all. The ones that didn't cross are the ones we cannot see. This essay is about trying to see them anyway.

title: Grey City
description: Chennai photographed exclusively in the hours between 6am and 8am — the city before it becomes itself. Grey City is a portrait of a metropolis mid-transformation, caught between sleep and waking.

sections.title: Before light
sections.body: The city at 6am belongs to sweepers, to fisherwomen, to dogs. To the people whose work is done before the city wakes up to need it. I photographed them for a year without speaking to them. I was afraid of ruining something.

sections.title: Concrete grammar
sections.body: Chennai is a city that has not decided what it looks like. Old bungalows stand next to glass towers next to construction sites. Grey City is a grammar lesson in a language that is still being invented.

title: Salt Flat
description: The Rann of Kutch across four seasons — a landscape that is sea, then land, then salt, then nothing. Salt Flat is a meditation on geological time and the human communities that have worked this edge for centuries.

sections.title: The season of salt
sections.body: The agariyas arrive in October and leave in June. Eight months on the salt flat, in houses made of salt. The children grow up knowing a landscape that does not look like anywhere else on earth.

sections.title: White
sections.body: There are photographs here where the horizon disappears. Where the salt reflects the sky so completely that you cannot tell which way is up. I have not captioned these. I think they resist captioning.

title: Night Shift
description: A 55-minute observational video portrait of the night shift at a tea processing factory in Assam. No narration. Only the machines, the workers, the tea, and the 3am light through factory windows.

sections.title: Note
sections.body: Night Shift was filmed over six weeks, always between 10pm and 6am. The factory produces 4,000 kilograms of processed tea per night. The workers earn ₹280 per shift. I have not disguised these facts.

title: Flood Plain
description: An image essay tracking three families across five years of annual flooding on the Brahmaputra flood plain. Each year the river takes something. Each year they return. A document of extraordinary ordinary resilience.

sections.title: The families
sections.body: Three families. Five years. Thirty-seven floods. I have changed their names but not their faces. They asked me not to hide them. They said: we are not ashamed of the river.

sections.title: What floods leave
sections.body: The Brahmaputra deposits as much as it takes. The silt is extraordinary — farmers speak of it the way wine people speak of terroir. The problem is you cannot plan for it. The river decides when and how much.

title: Red Earth
description: The iron ore mines of Bellary, Karnataka — a landscape transformed into raw material. Red Earth photographs the mines, the miners, the trucks, and the red dust that settles on everything within 20 kilometres.

sections.title: The colour
sections.body: Everything in Bellary is the same colour. The earth, the trucks, the shirts of the men who drive them, the inside of their lungs. Red Earth is a monochrome book that happens to be full of colour.

sections.title: After the ban
sections.body: In 2011 the Supreme Court banned illegal mining in Bellary. The book contains photographs from before and after. The difference is not what you expect.

title: Paper Town
description: An essay on the last offset printing presses of Pune's Narayan Peth neighbourhood — a district that once printed everything from schoolbooks to wedding cards to underground pamphlets. Now down to eleven shops.

sections.title: Eleven shops
sections.body: In 1988 there were 340 printing presses in Narayan Peth. Today there are eleven. This essay was printed on one of them, on a machine that is older than I am.

sections.title: The smell of ink
sections.body: Offset ink smells like a particular kind of effort. The men who run these shops have the ink in their skin. They say they can smell a badly set page. I believe them.

title: The Crossing
description: A 28-minute video work filmed entirely at the Wagah border ceremony over one year — 365 sunsets, compressed into 28 minutes. The ceremony as theatre. The theatre as document. The document as question.

sections.title: On repetition
sections.body: I filmed the Wagah ceremony every day for a year. The ceremony is identical each time. And yet it is never the same. The crowd changes. The light changes. The soldiers age imperceptibly. The flag does not.

title: Winter Notes
description: Shimla in winter — the tourists gone, the colonial architecture visible without the crowd. Winter Notes photographs the empty Mall Road, the shuttered hotels, the Himalayan light on British-era buildings built for a different sky.

sections.title: December
sections.body: Shimla in December is a different city. The Mall Road belongs to residents and monkeys. The Christ Church is locked. The Vice-Regal Lodge looks like what it is — a building that arrived from somewhere else and never quite settled.

sections.title: The light problem
sections.body: The British built Shimla for British light — grey, diffuse, flattering to Gothic stonework. The Himalayan light is entirely different. It is too clear. It shows everything. Winter Notes is partly about what happens when a built environment meets the wrong light.
